message:
fcp-cbor: split DepthExceeded out of PayloadTooLarge for adversarial-input telemetry

The recursion guard in `canonicalize_value_in_place` was reusing
`SerializationError::PayloadTooLarge`:

    return Err(SerializationError::PayloadTooLarge {
        len: depth,
        max: MAX_CANONICALIZATION_DEPTH,
    });

Two distinct failure modes share one error variant:

* "Encoded payload exceeded MAX_CANONICAL_OBJECT_BYTES (64 MiB)" —
  what the message text describes, what `to_canonical_cbor`/
  `deserialize_unchecked`/`serialize` raise on real size violations.
* "Value tree was nested deeper than MAX_CANONICALIZATION_DEPTH (128)"
  — a recursion-stack guard against adversarial deeply-nested input.
  The variant was being constructed with `len = depth = 130` and
  `max = MAX_CANONICALIZATION_DEPTH = 128`, so the Display read
  "payload too large (130 bytes > 128 bytes)" — wrong on both axes.

Operators triaging an adversarial payload could not tell from logs or
typed callers which guard tripped. Anyone trying to apply a different
mitigation (raising the size cap vs flattening the schema) was looking
at a misleading error.

Add a `DepthExceeded { depth: usize, max: usize }` variant with its own
Display ("canonicalization depth {depth} exceeds limit {max}") and
return it from the depth check. `PayloadTooLarge` keeps its meaning at
all real call sites in fcp-cbor, fcp-core/object, and fcp-protocol/
session — none of those construct PayloadTooLarge for depth.

Test updates:

* Three existing depth-boundary tests (`canonicalize_exceeding_max_depth_fails`,
  `depth_boundary_exceeding_max_fails`, `canonicalize_tag_exceeding_max_depth_fails`)
  swap their `matches!(.., PayloadTooLarge { .. })` for the new
  `matches!(.., DepthExceeded { max, .. } if max == MAX_CANONICALIZATION_DEPTH)`
  pattern so a future regression that conflates the two stops compiling.
* New `depth_exceeded_carries_actual_depth_and_is_distinct_from_payload_too_large`
  asserts the actual `depth` field round-trips and that the Display
  string contains "canonicalization depth", which is the substring
  operators will grep for in logs.

Verified locally with rch CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/fcp-cc1:
* `cargo test -p fcp-cbor --lib` → 450 passed (+1 new), 0 failed
* `cargo clippy -p fcp-cbor --lib -- -D warnings` → clean
* `cargo test -p fcp-core --lib` → 3911 passed
* `cargo test -p fcp-protocol --lib` → 453 passed

Out of scope, recorded after audit:

* The scratch_cap on `canonicalize_map`
  (`entries.len().saturating_mul(32).min(MAX_CANONICAL_OBJECT_BYTES)`)
  bounds only the *initial* `Vec::with_capacity` reservation. The
  scratch buffer can still grow up to the input size as keys are
  encoded, and the parallel `with_keys: Vec<(Range<usize>, Value, Value)>`
  is preallocated to `entries.len()` without a comparable cap. Total
  in-flight memory during canonicalization is bounded by the
  64 MiB input cap upstream of `to_canonical_cbor`/`deserialize_unchecked`,
  not by the scratch_cap heuristic — the heuristic only prevents a
  worst-case 1 GiB upfront reservation when the input could otherwise
  produce 32 M tiny entries. That is the property the original 2ot4f
  patch was after, and the audit confirms it holds.
* `Value::Tag` canonicalization recurses into the inner value with
  depth+1 and re-emits the tag verbatim. Tag 24 ("encoded CBOR data
  item") wraps a byte string whose contents are not re-canonicalized.
  No FCP type currently constructs `Value::Tag` (`grep -rn` finds
  only test fixtures), so the path is reachable only from
  attacker-supplied CBOR; `deserialize`'s round-trip check still
  catches non-canonical encodings outside the tag wrapper.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
